title: Ensure that the --service-account-extend-token-expiration parameter is set to false

input_row.title: Ensure that the --service-account-extend-token-expiration parameter is set to false
input_row.description: By default Kubernetes extends service account token lifetimes to one year to aid in transition from the legacy token settings.
input_row.rationale: This default setting is not ideal for security as it ignores other settings related to maximum token lifetime and means that a lost or stolen credential could be valid for an extended period of time. Impact: Disabling this setting means that the service account token expiry set in the cluster will be enforced, and service account tokens will expire at the end of that time frame.
input_row.audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --service-account-extend-token-expiration argument is set to false.
input_row.remediation: Edit the API server pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml on the Control Plane node and set the --service-account-extend-token-expiration parameter to false. --service-account-extend-token-expiration=false Default Value: By default, this parameter is set to true References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kube- apiserver/ 1.3 Controller Manager This section contains recommendations relating to Controller Manager configuration flags
input_row.impact: Disabling this setting means that the service account token expiry set in the cluster will be enforced, and service account tokens will expire at the end of that time frame.
input_row.references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kube- apiserver/ 1.3 Controller Manager This section contains recommendations relating to Controller Manager configuration flags

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_api_server_service_account_extend_token_expiration_disabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Query the kube-apiserver configuration to verify the value of the --service-account-extend-token-expiration flag.
- For clusters using static pod manifests (common in kubeadm and many managed Kubernetes distributions), inspect the /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml file for the presence and value of the --service-account-extend-token-expiration argument.
- If the cluster exposes the kube-apiserver Pod as a Kubernetes resource (e.g., via kube-system namespace), use:
  ```
  kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o yaml
  ```
  and inspect the command/args section for the flag.
- Alternatively, use:
  ```
  kubectl -n kube-system describe pod <kube-apiserver-pod-name>
  ```
  and look for the --service-account-extend-token-expiration argument.
- Pass Criteria: The flag --service-account-extend-token-expiration must be explicitly set to false.
- Fail Criteria: The flag is missing (defaults to true) or is set to true.
- Example check logic:
  - Parse the kube-apiserver manifest or Pod spec.
  - Locate the --service-account-extend-token-expiration argument.
  - Confirm its value is false.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.11 PDF
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification as Automated is correct. The audit can be performed using Kubernetes CLI commands to inspect the kube-apiserver configuration, which aligns with an automated approach.
- This check can be effectively conducted via Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands, as described in the assessment.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly accurate. The commands provided for inspecting the kube-apiserver configuration are valid and commonly used methods.
- The use of `kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o yaml` and `kubectl -n kube-system describe pod <kube-apiserver-pod-name>` are appropriate for checking the kube-apiserver Pod specifications.
- The program name `k8s_api_server_service_account_extend_token_expiration_disabled` is descriptive and follows a reasonable convention, though it could be slightly improved for clarity.
- The assessment correctly identifies the pass and fail criteria based on the flag's presence and value.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- The program name could be improved for clarity. A suggestion is `k8s_api_server_service_account_token_expiration_check` to better reflect the focus on checking the token expiration setting.
- Consider adding a step to verify if the kube-apiserver is running as a static pod or managed by a different mechanism, which might affect where the configuration is stored.
- It might be beneficial to include a note about checking for this setting in managed Kubernetes services, where direct access to the kube-apiserver configuration might not be available.
- An alternative method could involve using Kubernetes SDKs for more programmatic checks, though this is not strictly necessary given the current approach.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-structured and uses appropriate methods for the audit. The technical details are sound, and the approach is consistent with Kubernetes security best practices.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- Modify: Improve the program name for clarity as suggested.
- Keep the approach as Automated, as it is appropriate for this type of check.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be performed using Kubernetes CLI commands to inspect the kube-apiserver configuration, which supports an automated approach. The commands provided are valid and commonly used methods for checking the specified parameter, making it feasible to automate this compliance control.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

k8s_api_server_service_account_extend_token_expiration_disabled

- API/CLI Method: kubectl

- Fail Criteria: The flag is missing (defaults to true) or is set to true.
